title: Cat Walking Like Drunk? Ataxia Guide & Emergency Triage
meta_desc: If your cat has a wobbly, drunk-like gait, it could be ataxia. This is a high-priority emergency. Learn the causes and next steps for your cat.
content_html:
TL;DR: A sudden wobbly gait in cats is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention. Capturing a video of your cat's movement can help the medical team quickly identify the cause, which may range from poisoning to neurological injury.

What causes my cat to have a wobbly gait or walk like they are drunk?

When a cat walks as if they are "drunk," stumbling, or swaying, it is medically referred to as ataxia. This condition occurs when there is a lack of muscle coordination caused by an underlying issue in the sensory system, the vestibular system (inner ear), or the central nervous system. It can manifest as head tilting, limb weakness, or a complete inability to stand.

Is a sudden wobbly gait in my cat considered a medical emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for a sudden wobbly gait is High. Immediate action is required because:

  • Sudden uncoordination may be caused by acute poisoning, such as from lilies or antifreeze.
  • The condition could indicate a stroke, a severe inner ear infection, or a traumatic spinal injury.
  • Because these conditions can progress rapidly, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How do videos or photos help a veterinarian triage my cat's movement issues?

  • Capturing a short video helps the vet identify distinct markers for cerebellar, vestibular, or sensory ataxia.
  • Seeing the specific movement pattern allows the medical team to prioritize care and triage more effectively.
  • Visual evidence helps narrow down the cause even before the physical exam is finished.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ataxia in cats, characterized by incoordination and an appearance of being "drunk," can stem from several causes. Cerebellar dysfunction, suggested by asynergia and dysmetria, can manifest as a loss of equilibrium when moving or jumping. Feline panleukopenia virus (FPV) can cause cerebellar hypoplasia, leading to incoordination and tremors, although passive transfer of antibodies from queens has made this less common. Certain genetic disorders, such as α-Mannosidosis, primarily seen in cats, may also cause neurologic deficits with cerebellar signs being the most consistent feature.

Chapter: Neurology, Genetics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1235)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of a drunk-like gait?
faqs.answer: Common causes include ingestion of toxins, severe ear infections, neurological disorders, spinal trauma, or metabolic issues like low blood sugar.

faqs.question: Should I wait to see if my cat's balance improves?
faqs.answer: No. Because a wobbly gait often indicates a serious neurological or toxicological event, waiting can lead to permanent damage or life-threatening complications.

faqs.question: How can I keep my cat safe while traveling to the vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a well-padded carrier to prevent them from falling or injuring themselves further during transport.
